articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

more.title: Air Zimbabwe’s London route a game-changer for tourism, says PS
more.description: Nqobile Bhebhe nqobile.bhebhe@chronicle.co.zw PERMANENT Secretary in the Ministry of Tourism and Hospitality Industry, Dr Takaruza Munyanyiwa, has urged tourism industry players to aggressively market Air Zimbabwe as part of the country's destination offering, describing the resumption of direct flights between Harare and London after a 14-year hiatus as a landmark achievement that will transform Zimbabwe's tourism prospects. Speaking during consultations on the National Tourism Sector Strategy (NTSS) 2026-2030, Dr Munyanyiwa said the strategy was being crafted at a pivotal moment, marked by significant policy and infrastructure developments that would strengthen Zimbabwe's competitiveness as a tourism destination. Air Zimbabwe is expected to deploy a leased 302-seater Airbus A330 aircraft on the route. Dr Munyanyiwa said the restoration of the Harare-London service was a major milestone that deserved national recognition. I want to say the NTSS is happening at a very crucial time with our national airline, Air Zimbabwe resuming London flight today and this is 14 years later. This is a major milestone and we need to applaud Government for getting us here. NTSS is a defining moment in our collective journey to shape the future of Zimbabwe's tourism sector. This is why we are here, said Dr Munyanyiwa. He noted that having a functioning national airline placed Zimbabwe among a select group of African countries with such strategic assets. Just to put into context, out of the 54 countries in Africa there are 14 national airlines so we are among the best. Dr Munyanyiwa said the direct London service would fundamentally change how Zimbabwe positions itself in international tourism markets, as travellers increasingly prefer destinations that offer seamless air connectivity. What it means now is as you package your products, our story from today is different because we have had tourists coming here via different routes, tourists want a direct flight. You cannot be a principal destination if you do not have direct connectivity. Our national airline is flying that route (London), it means we can provide connectivity to the region and also what we need now is a robust domestic connectivity arrangement, he said. He challenged tourism operators to incorporate Air Zimbabwe into their travel packages and marketing campaigns to maximise the benefits of the restored route.

more.title: ZRP launches anti-crime campaign in Bulawayo
more.description: Gibson Mhaka gibson.mhaka@bmetro.co.zw THE Zimbabwe Republic Police (ZRP) on Thursday will roll out a national anti-crime campaign in Bulawayo aimed at strengthening the fight against drug and substance abuse, stock theft, sexual offences and other criminal activities through enhanced community participation. The campaign, to be held at the Large City Hall, is expected to bring together members of the public, community leaders, business representatives, youths and other stakeholders as police intensify efforts to promote safer communities. Held under the theme A Safer Nation Starts With Us, the campaign seeks to reinforce the message that crime prevention is a shared responsibility requiring the active participation of every citizen. The initiative comes as the police continue to encourage members of the public to work closely with law enforcement agencies by reporting criminal activities and suspicious behaviour in their communities. The campaign will focus on raising awareness about the dangers of drug and substance abuse, which has emerged as one of the country's most pressing social challenges, particularly among young people. Police will also engage communities on measures to curb stock theft, a crime that continues to threaten the livelihoods of many rural households, while educating the public on the prevention and reporting of sexual offences and other crimes. The event is expected to emphasise the importance of timely reporting of crime, with the police urging members of the public to embrace the See Something, Say Something approach as part of efforts to build a crime-free society. According to the event programme, the campaign will run under the rallying call: Working Together for a Crime-Free Society – Together We Can, Together We Will. The ZRP says community policing remains one of the most effective tools in combating crime, as it fosters trust and cooperation between law enforcement officers and the public. Members of the public have been encouraged to attend the campaign and interact with police officers on issues affecting the safety and security of their communities. The campaign forms part of the ZRP's nationwide drive to enhance public awareness, strengthen partnerships with communities and promote collective responsibility in preventing crime across the country.

more.title: Bulawayo family appeals for help to find missing elderly woman
more.description: Raymond Jaravaza, Zimpapers Reporter A BULAWAYO family is appealing to members of the public for assistance in locating a missing woman who disappeared almost two and a half years ago in February 2024. 65-year-old Nomthandazo Ndubiwa (65) disappeared from her home in Bulawayo after an argument over business dealings with her estranged husband, according to missing granny sister Mrs Nokuthula Zulu. My sister has been missing for a few years after she and her estranged husband disagreed over the handling of finances from a motor spares business that they were operating together, The couples' daughter who was financing her parents' joint business venture passed away in the UK in November 2022. Thats when the misunderstanding between the couple started until it led to be disappearance of the missing granny, according to Mrs Zulu. We have been trying to locate my sister to no avail since she left Zimbabwe to buy motor spares in Botswana with her then husband, said Mrs Zulu The two had been running a business together, which had been financed by their daughter as a way of supporting her parents so they had no choice but to work together even when their relationship and marriage was not in a good space, said Mrs Zulu. Police reports about the missing granny were made to authorities in Zimbabwe and Botswana about more than two years later a breakthrough into what happened to Ndubiwa remains elusive. The family seeks assistance to locate their loved one. We are traumatised as a family. My sister is an elderly woman who is missed by her family. We appeal to the public in both Zimbabwe and Botswana, where she was supposedly last believed to be headed to assist us, she said. Mrs Zulu said the husband of the missing granny is believed to be in the neighbouring country and has reportedly professed ignorance about the whereabouts of Ndubiwa.

more.title: Danish aquaculture technology heads to Zimbabwe
more.description: Theseus Mauruki Shambare ZIMBABWE'S aquaculture sector is set for a major technological upgrade following Government's backing of the importation of modern Danish fish farming equipment, a move expected to accelerate fish production and support the country's drive to attain fish self-sufficiency and build a US$1 billion fisheries industry. The Ministry of Agriculture, Mechanisation and Water Resources Development has endorsed Fin Fish Suppliers' application for an import duty and Value Added Tax (VAT) waiver on a full cage fish farming system valued at US$37 701.40 to be imported from Denmark. In a letter to the Ministry of Finance, Economic Development and Investment Promotion seen by Zimpapers reporters, Agriculture Secretary Professor Obert Jiri said the investment aligns with Government's strategy to expand aquaculture and strengthen food and nutrition security. He said fish farming had become a strategic pillar for Zimbabwe's economic revival. As you may be aware, fish farming is a strategic pillar for Zimbabwe's economic revival, food and nutrition security, said Prof Jiri. By diversifying agricultural exports, curbing malnutrition and creating rural employment, the sector directly aligns with national growth targets. In the letter, Prof Jiri said Zimbabwe's aquaculture industry was still developing and required targeted support to unlock its full potential. He said exempting the imported equipment from duty and VAT would lower the high capital costs associated with commercial fish farming, making the industry more accessible while stimulating investment. The equipment is a long-term investment that comes with ripple effects including job creation, increased fish production and support for national food and nutrition security, he said. The ministry said the project, to be implemented by Fin Fish Suppliers in Lake Kariba South, Binga District, has the capacity to produce up to 7 200 tonnes of fish annually. It said the investment supports the Agriculture and Food Systems Transformation Strategy, the Zimbabwe Tilapia Value Chain Market Strategy and the transboundary Lake Kariba Blue Economy Strategy. Responding to questions from Zimpapers, Fin Fish Suppliers director Mr Tinashe Chihota confirmed the authenticity of the ministry's letter and said the company had already taken delivery of the Danish cage system, which will soon be floated in Binga. We can confirm that the letter is authentic and we have already received the cage system. The next stage is to float it in Binga as we move into commercial production, he said. Mr Chihota said he appreciated Government's continued support for young entrepreneurs venturing into aquaculture, saying the enabling policy environment was giving youth-owned businesses confidence to invest in large-scale fish production. We sincerely appreciate how Government is supporting and empowering youth fish farmers. Such interventions lower investment costs and encourage young Zimbabweans to participate in the blue economy, he said. He said besides investing in cage fish production, Fin Fish Suppliers recently received another consignment of premium European Union-certified Aller Aqua fish feed from Denmark to ensure farmers have access to high-quality feed. Mr Chihota said the feed delivers an average feed conversion ratio of below 1.4, allowing farmers to reduce production costs by about 30 percent while achieving faster fish growth. Looking ahead, he said the company was eager to see the establishment of the planned cold-chain facility under Government and development partner support, saying it would strengthen fish marketing and reduce post-harvest losses. We are looking forward to the establishment of the cold-chain facility as Government and its development partners work towards increasing national fish production to 60 000 tonnes and achieving self-sufficiency, he said.
